headline: The Long Collapse Century: 2000 US Presidential Election

events.title: 2000 US Presidential Election
events.description: Constitutional crisis — no clear president until February 2001
events.explanation: Supreme Court ruling ignored, dueling electors, House decides

events.title: Dot-Com Crash Aftermath
events.description: Tech sector rebounds quickly; moderate rate cuts
events.explanation: Dot-com losses absorbed faster than expected

events.title: September 11 Attacks

events.explanation: Earlier rate normalization or better risk management prevents duration mismatch

events.title: ISS Transition

events.explanation: Commercial stations delayed; ISS deorbited on schedule leaving capability gap

events.title: US TikTok Ban Saga
events.description: Ban legislation passes; legal challenges delay enforcement; uncertainty persists

events.explanation: Global health equity gains from COVID era prove ephemeral

events.title: 2024 US Presidential Election
events.description: Harris wins; first female and Black/South Asian president

events.title: Longevity Research Boom

events.title: Brain-Computer Interface Trials
events.description: Military invests heavily; soldier augmentation program drives BCI development

events.title: Starship Reaches Orbit
events.description: Blue Origin or Chinese equivalent achieves super-heavy reusability first
events.explanation: New Glenn or Long March 9 beat Starship to market; competition reshapes industry

events.title: Grid Modernization Crisis
events.description: Cascading grid failures; major blackouts drive political backlash against renewables

events.title: AI Regulation
events.description: Minimal regulation; market-driven approach

events.title: Climate Tipping Points
events.description: Major tipping point crossed; accelerating warming

events.title: Post-Quantum Cryptography Transition
events.description: Managed migration to post-quantum standards with minimal disruption

events.title: Artemis Lunar Landing
events.description: Artemis lunar landing slips to 2030 due to technical and funding delays
events.explanation: HLS development challenges and congressional budget fights push timeline back

events.title: Taiwan Strait Crisis
events.description: Heightened tensions but no military action

events.title: Energy Transition
events.description: SMRs and fusion progress drive nuclear comeback

events.title: Alzheimer's Treatment Breakthrough
events.description: Blood biomarker test enables prevention; Alzheimer's incidence drops dramatically

events.title: AGI Threshold
events.description: AGI remains distant; narrow AI continues advancing

events.title: Hydrogen Economy Viability
events.description: Green hydrogen finds limited use in steel and shipping; not a broad solution

events.title: Second Pandemic
events.description: New pathogen detected and contained rapidly

events.title: Japan Population Crisis
events.description: PM Ishida's 'Graceful Contraction' white paper redefines GDP-per-capita as Japan's primary metric; 340 rural municipalities formally merge into regional cooperatives, closing 2,100 schools but tripling per-student spending in the remainder
events.explanation: Japan becomes the first major economy to explicitly plan for a smaller population; pension eligibility raised to 70, healthcare rationing introduced through 'quality-adjusted life years' scoring, and the yen strengthens as per-capita wealth rises

events.title: Space Tourism Mainstream
events.description: Virgin Galactic's Mojave terminal processes 40 flights per week; Blue Origin undercuts them at $89,000 a seat; 4,200 people reach space in a single year, more than all government astronauts in history combined
events.explanation: Reusable vehicle economics follow the airline playbook -- fill the seats, cut the price; three consecutive years without a fatality builds insurance industry confidence, and Lloyd's of London begins offering standard space tourism policies

events.title: US Political Realignment
events.description: The 117th through 121st Congresses pass fewer bills than any stretch since the Civil War; a government shutdown lasting 47 days in 2030 closes national parks during peak summer season, costing gateway communities $2.1 billion
events.explanation: Neither party can assemble a governing majority, but neither fractures either; the filibuster survives, gerrymandering entrenches safe seats, and the Supreme Court blocks every structural reform; America governs by executive order and continuing resolution

events.title: Biotechnology Regulation
events.description: Dubai, Singapore, and the Cayman Islands compete to become the 'biotech Switzerland' -- offshore clinics offer unregulated gene therapies to wealthy medical tourists while the EU and US maintain strict controls; a two-tier system of genetic medicine emerges
events.explanation: Regulatory fragmentation creates a global patchwork: a therapy legal in Bangkok is a felony in Berlin; patients with means fly to permissive jurisdictions while the uninsured wait years for approved treatments, and a grey-market mRNA therapy sold on Telegram causes 14 deaths in Brazil

events.title: Space Debris Cleanup Industry
events.description: ClearSpace removes 400 objects per year but SpaceX, Amazon, and China's Guo Wang constellation launch 6,000 new satellites annually; the debris count keeps climbing despite $8 billion in annual cleanup spending -- like bailing out a boat while the hole gets bigger
events.explanation: The technology works brilliantly in demos but cannot scale fast enough; each cleanup mission costs $4-8 million per object while launching new satellites costs a fraction of that, creating a perverse incentive structure that the market alone cannot resolve

events.title: AI Economic Displacement
events.description: Despite breathless predictions, only 4.3% of US jobs actually automated by 2031; a Harvard Business School study finds that 60% of enterprise AI deployments stall during integration with legacy systems, and 'AI transformation' budgets quietly become IT maintenance budgets
events.explanation: The gap between demo and deployment turns out to be enormous -- hospitals can't connect AI diagnostics to their 1990s-era records systems, law firms discover AI hallucinations in briefs, and unions in France and Japan negotiate go-slow adoption clauses

events.title: Digital Currency Landscape
events.description: 114 nations operate CBDCs by 2032; the digital yuan's 'smart contract' feature -- which can restrict spending to approved categories -- is copied by 30 governments; India's digital rupee processes 2.8 billion transactions daily, and the ECB's digital euro launches with 200 million users in its first quarter
events.explanation: China's 8-year head start proves decisive: the digital yuan's infrastructure becomes the template, and its cross-border payment system (mBridge) handles $4.3 trillion in annual trade settlement, eroding the dollar's dominance in Asian commerce; programmable money gives governments unprecedented fiscal precision but also unprecedented surveillance capability

events.title: China Demographic Reckoning
events.description: A pilot program to attract 500,000 Southeast Asian workers to Yunnan and Guangxi provinces collapses after anti-foreigner riots in Kunming and a Weibo campaign with 800 million views demanding 'China for the Chinese'; only 23,000 visas are actually issued
events.explanation: The Han-centric national identity proves immovable: 91% of Chinese citizens oppose immigration in government polling, local officials sabotage integration programs, and the workers who do arrive face systematic discrimination that makes retention impossible; China confronts the reality that its demographic problem has no cultural shortcut

events.title: European Defense Architecture
events.description: NATO establishes a permanent 300,000-strong rapid reaction force stationed across Poland, the Baltics, and Romania; Germany's defense budget reaches $120 billion, making the Bundeswehr Europe's largest conventional force for the first time since reunification
events.explanation: The Ukraine war's legacy is a permanently mobilized Europe: joint command structures, interoperable weapons systems, and a new NATO Cyber Command in Tallinn staffed by 8,000 personnel transform the alliance from a political framework into a genuinely integrated military machine

events.title: Lunar Base Establishment
events.description: Artemis IX's lunar lander develops a fuel leak during descent and aborts 3km above the surface; China's habitat-construction robot fails in the extreme cold of permanent shadow; both programs quietly push permanent base timelines to 'no earlier than 2040' while maintaining crewed fly-by and short-stay missions
events.explanation: The engineering reality of sustaining human life on the Moon -- radiation shielding, dust contamination, cryogenic fuel storage, and resupply logistics -- proves harder than either nation's optimistic timelines assumed; both programs remain active but at reduced ambition, and the Moon stays a place humans visit rather than live

events.title: AI Governance Framework
events.description: The Geneva AI Accord, signed in November 2032 by the US, China, EU, India, and 67 other nations, creates the International AI Safety Agency (IASA) headquartered in Singapore with authority to audit frontier models, mandate kill-switches, and suspend deployments; former Estonian PM Kaja Kallas appointed first director-general
events.explanation: Two catalysts force the deal: a near-catastrophic AI-generated bioweapon design leaked on 4chan in 2031, and China's calculation that international rules would constrain American private-sector advantages more than its own state-directed program; verification protocols modeled on nuclear inspections require compute-facility access

events.title: AI-Driven Scientific Discovery
events.description: A Stanford postdoc uses generative AI to fabricate 14 cancer biology papers with perfectly synthetic data, patient records, and microscopy images; the fraud goes undetected for 2 years and 6 clinical trials launch based on the false findings before a whistleblower AI-detection tool at PubMed flags statistical anomalies
events.explanation: The 'Stanford Fourteen' scandal triggers a crisis of confidence: Nature and Science retract 340 papers in a single year after deploying AI fraud-detection tools, and the NIH suspends $2.8 billion in grants pending verification; the irony that AI both enables and detects the fraud is lost on no one, and several countries begin requiring raw-data escrow for all published research

events.title: Global Tax Reform
events.description: 42 nations now levy a 3-8% tax on digital advertising revenue, cloud computing services, and AI API calls; India's version alone generates $14 billion annually from Google, Meta, and Amazon; the 'where value is created' principle finally sticks after Nigeria and Indonesia threaten to block non-compliant platforms entirely
events.explanation: The Global South coalition, led by India's finance minister Nirmala Sitharaman's successor Arun Khanna, reframes digital taxation as an anti-colonial issue: 'Our citizens' data creates their wealth; our treasuries deserve a share'; the US initially objects but acquiesces after its own digital services tax generates $28 billion from Chinese tech giants operating in America

events.title: Green Industrial Revolution
events.description: The 'Green Five' -- founders of CATL, First Solar, Northvolt, Reliance Clean Energy, and a Chilean lithium magnate -- hold combined wealth of $1.2 trillion; their patents on solid-state batteries and perovskite solar cells extract rents from every clean energy installation on Earth; the Global South pays licensing fees that exceed the development aid it receives
events.explanation: The clean energy transition replicates the oil economy's worst feature: concentrated ownership of essential infrastructure; patent thickets prevent competition, and developing nations that lack manufacturing capacity become dependent on imported green technology just as they were on imported oil, while the new green oligarchs wield political influence that dwarfs the old fossil fuel lobby

events.title: Global Urbanization Trajectory
events.description: Holographic telepresence and AI collaboration tools make physical offices irrelevant for 180 million knowledge workers globally; Boise, Galway, Chiang Mai, and Oaxaca become boomtowns as remote workers flee expensive, crowded metros; Manhattan office vacancy hits 38% and landlords convert towers to vertical farms and micro-apartments
events.explanation: The AI-remote-work combination does what COVID only hinted at: when an AI assistant handles scheduling, note-taking, and follow-ups, and a holographic display makes remote meetings indistinguishable from in-person, the case for paying $4,000/month rent in San Francisco evaporates; smaller cities with good broadband, low costs, and high quality of life become the new centers of economic gravity

events.title: US Institutional Fracture
events.description: Congress passes the 'American Renewal Amendments' -- ranked-choice voting for federal offices, 18-year Supreme Court terms, and an independent redistricting commission -- after a bipartisan 'Save the Republic' coalition led by former presidents and military leaders makes reform a patriotic cause; ratification by 38 states completes in 11 months, the fastest since the 26th Amendment
events.explanation: The near-death experience of the secession crisis concentrates minds: business leaders, military officers, and religious figures form an unlikely alliance that frames institutional reform as patriotism rather than partisanship; the reforms don't eliminate disagreement but they restore the legitimacy of the process for resolving it

events.title: Post-Oil Economy Transition
events.description: Global oil demand climbs back to 95 million barrels/day as India's industrial boom, Africa's urbanization, and stubborn aviation and shipping demand overwhelm EV displacement; ExxonMobil's stock triples from its 2030 low, and the 'peak oil demand' narrative is quietly retired from conference agendas
events.explanation: The math of 3 billion people in South and Southeast Asia entering the consumer class for the first time defeats the math of rich-world electrification: India alone adds 40 million vehicles annually, most of them ICE; petrochemical demand for plastics, fertilizers, and synthetic materials grows 4% per year, and synthetic aviation fuel proves too expensive to scale

events.title: Arctic Ice-Free Summers
events.description: September 2033 Arctic sea ice extent drops below 1 million km2 -- the threshold scientists define as 'ice-free' -- matching the median projection from IPCC AR7; the moment arrives not with a bang but with a satellite data update that makes the front page of every newspaper on Earth for exactly one day before being displaced by other news
events.explanation: The Arctic has been warming at 4x the global rate since 2000, and the ice-albedo feedback loop -- dark water absorbs more heat than reflective ice, melting more ice, exposing more dark water -- makes the trajectory essentially irreversible; the milestone is psychologically devastating but scientifically expected, a slow-motion catastrophe arriving precisely on schedule

events.title: Antibiotic Resistance Crisis
events.description: MIT's AI drug discovery platform identifies Teixobactin-2, the first genuinely novel antibiotic class in 40 years, effective against all tested gram-negative resistant bacteria; Roche pays $8.2 billion for the manufacturing rights; separately, the CARB-X global fund's 'market entry reward' of $2 billion per approved antibiotic finally makes the economics work for pharma companies
events.explanation: The breakthrough is as much financial as scientific: antibiotics were unprofitable because they're used sparingly and briefly (unlike chronic disease drugs), so the 'Netflix model' subscription payment -- where governments pay a fixed annual fee for unlimited access regardless of usage -- gives companies guaranteed revenue without incentivizing overuse

events.title: Global Food System Shock
events.description: The Midwest heat dome dissipates after 3 weeks rather than the feared 6, and the Indian monsoon arrives late but delivers 85% of normal rainfall; global crop losses reach 12% -- painful but manageable; commodity prices spike briefly then settle as markets recognize the harvest is bruised but not broken
events.explanation: Climate luck plays a role -- the jet stream pattern shifts before inflicting maximum damage -- but so does systemic resilience: crop diversification, improved soil management, and satellite-guided precision irrigation allow farmers to adapt in real time; the food system absorbs a significant shock without cascading failure, vindicating decades of agricultural research investment

events.title: Kessler Syndrome Risk
events.description: A Chinese anti-satellite weapons test gone wrong at 850km triggers an uncontrollable cascade that spreads across all LEO altitudes within 8 months; 4,200 active satellites destroyed; GPS degrades to 200m accuracy then fails entirely; weather forecasting reverts to 1970s capability; the night sky fills with streaking debris visible to the naked eye -- beautiful and terrifying
events.explanation: The cascade, once started above 500km where atmospheric drag cannot clear debris, becomes self-sustaining: each collision creates thousands of fragments, each fragment a potential impactor; LEO becomes impassable for an estimated 40-80 years; humanity effectively loses access to low orbit, crippling telecommunications, navigation, earth observation, and military surveillance simultaneously

events.title: Israel-Palestine Resolution
events.description: The tenth anniversary of the 2023-24 Gaza war passes with a memorial in Gaza City and a security cabinet meeting in Jerusalem; periodic IDF raids in Jenin and Tulkarm, occasional rocket fire from Gaza, settlement construction continuing at 8,000 units/year; the conflict has become background noise, a permanent low-grade wound that neither kills the patient nor heals
events.explanation: Both leaderships find the status quo preferable to the risks of change: Israeli governments gain votes from settlement expansion, Palestinian leaders maintain international sympathy and aid flows as victims, and the human cost -- 200-400 deaths per year, thousands detained, economic stagnation -- is borne by ordinary people whose suffering has become geopolitically invisible

events.title: Lunar Economy Begins
events.description: After 3 years of operations, Intuitive Machines publishes a sobering analysis: lunar water extraction costs $180,000 per kilogram when accounting for equipment replacement, power systems, and dust-related maintenance failures -- roughly 100x more expensive than launching water from Earth on a Starship; the company's stock drops 82% in a single day
events.explanation: The lunar environment proves far more hostile to sustained industrial operations than optimists assumed: abrasive regolith dust destroys seals and bearings, the 14-day lunar night requires massive battery systems, and radiation degrades electronics in months rather than years; the Moon remains scientifically valuable but economically barren, a harsh lesson in the difference between 'technically possible' and 'commercially viable'

events.title: African Union Evolution
events.description: AU peacekeepers operate in 7 active conflict zones from Mali to Mozambique; the 'African Standby Force' reaches 65,000 troops with Nigerian and South African brigades as its backbone; a drone-surveillance network monitoring the Sahel, funded by a joint EU-AU program, reduces insurgent attacks by 40% but raises civil liberties concerns among human rights groups
events.explanation: The security crisis absorbs all institutional energy: with Sahel jihadism spreading, the Horn of Africa destabilized by climate migration, and Great Lakes conflicts flaring again, the AU becomes primarily a military coordination body; economic integration stalls as borders harden for security reasons, and the continent's young population sees more soldiers than entrepreneurs

events.title: Arctic Governance Framework
events.description: Shell, Equinor, Rosneft, and CNOOC all begin exploratory drilling in disputed waters; Canadian and Danish naval vessels confront each other near Hans Island over newly accessible mineral deposits; an unregulated fishing fleet of 200 Chinese trawlers depletes newly ice-free waters off Svalbard, devastating cod and halibut stocks within 2 seasons
events.explanation: The absence of binding governance creates a classic tragedy of the commons: every actor has an incentive to extract resources quickly before rules are imposed, and the environmental damage compounds -- oil spill cleanup in Arctic waters is essentially impossible with current technology, and overfishing in newly productive waters depletes stocks before they can be assessed or managed

events.title: Ectogenesis Research
events.description: Neonatal BioBag systems, deployed in 340 hospitals across 28 countries, increase survival rates for 22-week premature infants from 10% to 78%; a premature baby born at 21 weeks in Melbourne is sustained in an artificial womb for 14 weeks and goes home healthy -- but human ectogenesis from conception remains prohibited everywhere and technically unfeasible

events.title: Deep Sea Mining
events.description: A coalition of 34 nations led by Chile, France, and Palau pushes through a 30-year moratorium at the International Seabed Authority after a Greenpeace expedition films the devastation left by pilot mining operations: seafloor sediment plumes visible from space, destroyed sponge gardens that were 10,000 years old, and a 90% decline in deep-sea fish within mining zones
events.explanation: The viral footage -- narrated by David Attenborough in his final public appearance before retirement at 109 -- generates enough public outrage to overcome industry lobbying; BMW, Volvo, and Samsung join the moratorium campaign to protect their green brand images, and recycling advances reduce virgin mineral demand enough to make the ban economically survivable

events.title: China Post-Xi Transition
events.description: Vice Premier Chen Wenqing, Xi's former security chief, assumes the presidency and general secretaryship in a transition so seamless it appears choreographed years in advance; his first act is to purge 3 Politburo members suspected of reform sympathies; his second is to announce a 15% increase in military spending with explicit reference to 'completing national reunification'
events.explanation: Xi built a succession mechanism modeled on his own rise: a loyalist installed in key security and party positions years before the transition, with personal files on every potential rival; Chen's background in intelligence and internal security signals that the next era will prioritize control and nationalism over economic reform, doubling down on the model Xi created

events.title: Carbon Capture at Scale
events.description: A team at Tsinghua University discovers an engineered enzyme -- inspired by ocean phytoplankton -- that captures CO2 at $28/tonne using seawater and sunlight; the 'Tsinghua Process' can be deployed on floating platforms across tropical oceans; a pilot system off Hainan Island captures 50,000 tonnes in its first year of operation with near-zero energy input
events.explanation: The biological approach sidesteps the energy penalty that plagues mechanical systems: the enzyme does the thermodynamic work that fans, heaters, and compressors do in conventional plants, using ambient solar energy; the captured carbon precipitates as calcium carbonate that sinks to the ocean floor; if scaled to 10 million floating platforms, the system could remove 10 gigatonnes annually at a cost comparable to global military spending

events.title: Neural Interface Technology
events.description: Despite billions in marketing, only 0.04% of the global population adopts neural interfaces; a Pew survey finds that 78% of Americans consider brain implants 'creepy' and 64% say they would 'never' get one; the technology joins Google Glass and 3D TV in the graveyard of innovations that worked technically but failed culturally
events.explanation: The brain turns out to be the one domain where humans draw a firm line against technological intrusion: smartphones were external and removable, but the idea of a permanent device inside the skull triggers deep evolutionary aversion; even non-invasive versions face skepticism after a viral story about a hacked consumer BCI that caused its user to experience phantom pain for 72 hours

events.title: European Demographic Challenge
events.description: The 'Patriotic Alliance' coalition governing Italy, Austria, and Hungary pushes through the 'European Border Compact' cutting legal immigration by 80%; Polish farmers cannot harvest their crops and recruit Ukrainian workers illegally; German hospitals close 1,200 wards for lack of nurses; the EU's GDP growth turns negative for the first time outside a crisis as the workforce simply shrinks faster than productivity can compensate
events.explanation: The far-right discovers that you cannot have a welfare state without workers: pension funds require contributors, hospitals require staff, and farms require hands; but the political coalition that won on anti-immigration sentiment cannot reverse course without losing its base, creating a doom loop of labor shortage, economic decline, and voter anger that the incumbents channel into more immigration restriction

events.title: ASEAN Integration Trajectory
events.description: The ASEAN Economic Community 2.0 eliminates tariffs on 98% of goods, establishes mutual professional license recognition, and launches the 'ASEAN Digital Single Market' with unified data standards; a Thai software engineer can work in Vietnam without a visa, an Indonesian startup can sell across all 10 markets with one registration, and intra-ASEAN trade hits $1.2 trillion
events.explanation: The integration is driven by a generation of Southeast Asian entrepreneurs who grew up seeing the EU's single market as the model and China's Belt and Road as the threat: deeper ASEAN integration provides the scale to compete with both giants, and the digital economy -- where ASEAN's 700 million young, tech-savvy consumers are the prize -- provides the economic incentive that older industries lacked

events.title: Fusion Energy Progress
events.description: SPARC-2 achieves net energy gain in the lab but the reactor's tungsten first-wall components degrade 5x faster than predicted under sustained neutron bombardment; Commonwealth Fusion Systems pushes its commercial timeline from 2040 to 'early 2050s'; ITER in southern France finally achieves first plasma in 2036 -- 16 years behind schedule and $28 billion over budget
events.explanation: The materials problem proves intractable in the near term: no known substance can withstand years of 14 MeV neutron bombardment without becoming brittle and radioactive; the physics works, the engineering works for short pulses, but sustained commercial operation requires materials science breakthroughs that have not yet occurred; fusion remains the energy source of the future, and always will be -- at least for another 20 years

events.title: Northern Sea Route
events.description: The International Maritime Organization, backed by an EU-US-Japan-Korea coalition, declares the Northern Sea Route an international strait under UNCLOS Article 38; Russia protests but acquiesces after a trade deal offers compensation: Western technology for Russian Arctic oil fields in exchange for open transit rights; a multilateral Arctic Shipping Authority headquartered in Tromsø manages traffic, safety, and environmental standards
events.explanation: The legal argument is strong -- international law provides for transit passage through straits used for international navigation -- and the economic pressure is stronger: shipping companies representing 60% of global tonnage refuse to pay Russian monopoly fees, and the threat of rerouting through the Northwest Passage (now seasonally viable through Canadian waters) gives Russia an incentive to negotiate rather than dictate

events.title: Latin America Political Direction
events.description: President Washington launches the '$500 Billion Americas Partnership' -- the largest US investment in the hemisphere since the Marshall Plan -- including semiconductor fabs in Mexico, green hydrogen plants in Chile, and digital infrastructure across Central America; the program is explicitly pitched as an alternative to Chinese Belt and Road: 'We forgot our neighborhood; we won't make that mistake again'
events.explanation: China's growing influence -- $280 billion in regional investment, trade agreements with every major economy, and a naval facility in Argentina -- finally triggers the US strategic response that decades of Latin American lobbying could not; the competition benefits the region directly, as both powers bid for influence with investment rather than coercion, and Latin American nations skillfully play the two suitors against each other

events.title: Human Genetic Enhancement Debate
events.description: The 'Oviedo Protocol on Human Genetic Integrity,' signed by 92 nations at the Council of Europe, bans embryo selection for non-medical traits and mandates 10-year prison sentences for violators; enforcement is strict in the EU and Japan but nonexistent in Singapore, the UAE, and several Caribbean nations that market themselves as 'reproductive freedom' destinations

events.title: Genetic Privacy
events.description: Despite GINA protections in the US, employers use 'wellness programs' that require genetic screening as a condition of health insurance discounts; insurers in India, Brazil, and most of Africa openly price policies based on polygenic risk scores; a class-action lawsuit in Texas reveals that a major employer rejected 2,400 job applicants based on genetic predisposition data purchased from a consumer genealogy company
events.explanation: Genetic discrimination operates through loopholes and grey markets: life insurance (not covered by GINA), employment 'wellness' programs (technically voluntary), and data brokers who strip identifying information but sell genetic risk profiles that can be re-identified using publicly available genealogy databases; the result is a genetic underclass of people with high-risk profiles who cannot get insurance, jobs, or mortgages, punished for a future disease they may never develop

events.title: Space Resource Extraction
events.description: The US Strategic Minerals Reserve Act designates lunar and asteroid rare earths as 'critical national resources' and subsidizes extraction at $40,000/kg -- roughly 4x market price -- to reduce dependence on Chinese rare earth monopolies; the Pentagon's 'Orbital Forge' program funds in-space manufacturing of satellite components from asteroid metals to create a defense supply chain beyond Chinese reach
events.explanation: The strategic logic is straightforward: China controls 80% of global rare earth processing, and in a Taiwan crisis could cut off supplies overnight; space-sourced alternatives, while far more expensive, provide insurance against supply disruption; the subsidy is not economically rational but strategically necessary, much like the US government subsidized domestic semiconductor manufacturing in the 2020s

events.title: Autonomous Weapons Systems
events.description: The 'Vienna Protocol on Autonomous Weapons' bans offensive autonomous systems but permits 'defensive' and 'supervised' ones; the US, China, and Russia all sign, then classify their autonomous fighters as 'defensive,' their killer drones as 'human-supervised,' and their autonomous submarine hunters as 'mine countermeasures' -- every prohibited category has an approved synonym
events.explanation: The treaty is simultaneously a diplomatic achievement and a masterclass in bad faith: the text bans exactly the weapons every signatory already has, using definitions so narrow that simple relabeling achieves compliance; the Campaign to Stop Killer Robots calls it 'worse than no treaty at all, because it creates the illusion of governance while legitimizing everything it claims to prohibit'

events.title: Cislunar Economy
events.description: A single fuel depot at L1 operates at 30% capacity; only 8 missions per year justify the cislunar route rather than direct-to-Moon launches; the infrastructure exists but mostly sits idle, maintained by a skeleton crew of 2 at the depot, waiting for the demand that justifies its construction -- a space-age version of a highway built to a town that never grew
events.explanation: The cislunar infrastructure was built on projections of 50+ missions per year that assumed a booming lunar economy; when lunar mining and tourism develop slower than expected, the corridor has capacity for traffic that doesn't exist; the depot's operating costs of $3 billion/year produce political pressure to either scale up activity or shut it down, and neither option has a clear advocate

events.title: Antibiotic Crisis Resolution
events.description: Dr. Fatima al-Rashidi's team at King Abdullah University isolates teixobactin-II from a deep-sea vent microbe near the Azores, the first genuinely new antibiotic class in 38 years. Phase III trials show 94% efficacy against pan-resistant Klebsiella.

events.title: US Infrastructure Reckoning
events.description: Blackstone Infrastructure Partners acquires the Pennsylvania Turnpike, the Port of Long Beach, and Chicago's water system in a single quarter. Tolls on I-95 hit $0.45 per mile; wealthy suburbs get fiber-optic water monitoring while Jackson, Mississippi still runs on lead pipes.

events.title: Mars Mission
events.description: After three engine test failures at Boca Chica and a congressional funding shortfall of $140 billion, NASA Administrator Singh announces 'a responsible delay' to 2053. The crew selection class of 2038, already trained and bonded, quietly disperses to university posts and consulting firms.

events.title: Ocean Acidification Status
events.description: Norwegian oceanographer Dr. Erik Halvorsen's fleet of 340 autonomous 'liming ships' disperses olivine powder across the North Atlantic, raising local pH by 0.12 units. Combined with a 2.8-million-km2 marine reserve network, the approach buys time but cannot reverse the underlying chemistry.

events.title: Synthetic Biology Maturity
events.description: After the Rotterdam garage-pathogen incident, a panicked UN General Assembly passes the Biological Research Restriction Treaty banning gene synthesis equipment without state license. Research labs in 90 countries shutter overnight; 14,000 PhD students find their dissertations illegal. China and Singapore refuse to sign, becoming the only hubs for legal synbio research.

events.title: China Political Trajectory
events.description: Protests over contaminated baby formula in Hebei province snowball into a nationwide movement after PLA garrison commander Colonel Zhao Mingfei refuses orders to deploy against civilians in Wuhan. The Standing Committee dissolves itself at 3 AM on a Wednesday; by morning, six different factions claim legitimacy.

events.title: Mars Communication Infrastructure
events.description: Only four of ten planned satellites launch before Congressional budget cuts freeze the program. Mars crews get 200 Mbps on good days but lose all contact for 14-day stretches during solar conjunction, forcing them to operate autonomously. Families describe the blackout periods as 'like your loved one dying and coming back every two years.'
events.explanation: Congressional appropriations committees slashed the interplanetary relay budget by 60%, citing cost overruns on the Mars mission itself. The partial constellation functions but leaves dangerous gaps.

events.title: Education Transformation
events.description: Parents in Tokyo, Helsinki, and Toronto revolt against screen-based learning, citing studies showing AI-tutored children score higher on tests but lower on social cognition and emotional resilience. Schools rebrand as 'human development centers' where children learn to argue, collaborate, and sit with boredom -- skills AI cannot teach.

events.title: Space Law Framework
events.description: In a secret meeting at the Shangri-La Hotel in Singapore, US and Chinese negotiators divide the inner solar system into 'zones of primary interest' -- effectively splitting the asteroid belt and lunar poles between them. The EU, India, and smaller spacefaring nations are furious but lack the launch capacity to contest the arrangement.

events.title: US Constitutional Amendment
events.description: The 35th state resolution stalls in the Wisconsin legislature by 3 votes after an aggressive lobbying campaign by tech companies and incumbent politicians. The amendment movement collapses into mutual recrimination between progressive and conservative reformers who cannot agree on what to fix, and the 237-year-old document endures unaltered.

events.title: Climate Refugee Treaty
events.description: The EU accepts climate migrants from North Africa under the Marseille Agreement; ASEAN creates a regional resettlement fund; the African Union opens internal borders. But no global framework exists, and 23 million displaced people in South Asia fall through every jurisdictional crack.

events.title: Arctic Extraction
events.description: Asteroid-mined platinum group metals undercut Arctic deposits by 80%; deep-sea mining in tropical waters proves cheaper and less politically fraught. The last Arctic oil platform, Gazprom's 'Prirazlomnaya-3,' shuts down after crude prices fall below extraction costs. The ice-free Arctic becomes a shipping lane rather than a mine.

events.title: Digital Nations
events.description: France leads a coalition of 90 nations passing the 'Westphalian Reaffirmation' at the UN, declaring sovereignty requires physical territory and 'a people rooted in place.' Digital nation leaders are arrested for tax evasion in six countries simultaneously; Prospera Global's server farms in Singapore are seized by the government at dawn.

events.title: Middle East New Economy
events.description: Saudi Arabia burns through its last sovereign wealth fund reserves; public sector salaries go unpaid for three months. Riots erupt in Jeddah and Dammam; migrant workers, stranded without exit visas or wages, storm the Indian embassy in Riyadh. Kuwait's ruling family flees to London. The petrodollar era ends not with a whimper but a riot.

events.title: EU Federation Decision
events.description: The European Defense Union deploys 280,000 troops under a single command structure led by French General Claire Moreau, while each nation keeps its own tax code, welfare system, and economic policy. The arrangement is modeled on NATO but with teeth -- and without the Americans, who quietly welcome European self-sufficiency.

events.title: Space Economy $1T
events.description: Nuclear thermal propulsion cuts Mars transit to 45 days and a space elevator prototype in Ecuador drops launch costs to $50 per kilogram. The space economy explodes to $2.4 trillion; AstroForge returns the first commercial platinum from asteroid Ryugu, crashing terrestrial platinum prices 60% and making every gold mine on Earth recalculate.

events.title: In-Space Manufacturing
events.description: Merck's orbital pharmaceutical lab grows protein crystals for three blockbuster drugs that cannot be manufactured terrestrially; TSMC's experimental space fab produces 200 wafers per month of radiation-hardened chips for spacecraft. Total orbital manufacturing revenue: $3.4 billion -- profitable but niche, like Swiss watchmaking in a digital age.
events.explanation: Only products where microgravity quality is irreplaceable justify the cost; the space manufacturing revolution remains real but narrow, serving specialized markets rather than transforming industry.

events.title: Cognitive Enhancement
events.description: After three N7 recipients in Seoul suffer catastrophic seizures from a firmware update, 67 nations ban neural implants for cognitive enhancement. The Vatican declares enhancement 'a violation of the soul's integrity'; Japan's Diet passes the 'Human Purity Act.' Underground enhancement clinics spring up in Bangkok and Tijuana, performing procedures in back rooms.

events.title: Currency Evolution
events.description: Global trade settles in roughly equal thirds: digital dollar for the Americas, digital yuan for Asia-Pacific, digital euro for Europe-Africa. The IMF's Special Drawing Rights basket expands to include all three plus the Indian rupee. Currency traders rejoice; economists warn that no single lender of last resort means the next financial crisis has no fire station.

events.title: Arms Control 2040s
events.description: The US deploys Mach 12 hypersonic glide vehicles on Ohio-class submarines; China tests an AI-commanded drone swarm of 10,000 units over the Taiwan Strait; Russia launches a nuclear-powered cruise missile with 'unlimited range.' Each development triggers the next in a spiral that burns $2.3 trillion annually and makes every city on Earth a potential target.

events.title: African Trade Integration
events.description: China owns or operates 34 African ports, 22,000 km of rail, and the continent's dominant mobile payment platform. When Zambia defaults on $14 billion in Chinese loans, Beijing seizes the Copperbelt's mining concessions. Tanzanian journalist Amina Mwangi's investigation 'The Dragon's Embrace' reveals that 73% of AfCFTA's infrastructure was built by Chinese state firms on Chinese terms.

events.title: Digital Immortality Attempt
events.description: Digital Korolyov passes every consciousness test devised by philosophers and neuroscientists: he reports subjective experiences, expresses novel preferences, and mourns his biological body. But there is no way to prove he is not an extraordinarily sophisticated p-zombie. Oxford philosopher Dame Rebecca Hartley publishes 'The Undecidable Self,' arguing that verification is logically impossible.

events.title: Mars Settlement
events.description: Ares Base operates like an Antarctic research station: 18-month rotations, strict psychological screening, and a supply ship every 26 months. Dr. Andersson's greenhouse produces 15% of food needs; the rest arrives from Earth at $40,000 per kilogram. Crew members describe the experience as 'simultaneously the loneliest and most purposeful place in the solar system.'

events.title: Asteroid Redirect Mission
events.description: Midas successfully parks the asteroid but the processing economics are brutal: extracting and returning platinum costs $48,000 per ounce versus $1,200 for terrestrial mining. Deep-sea mining and landfill reclamation using AI robotics prove far cheaper. The asteroid floats in cislunar space like a trillion-dollar savings account nobody can afford to withdraw from.
events.explanation: The fundamental problem is energy: refining ore in space requires either imported equipment or solar furnaces that take years to build. Meanwhile, Earth-based alternatives keep getting cheaper, moving the goalposts every year.

events.title: Latin American Integration
events.description: The Union of South American Nations launches with 8 member states, 480 million citizens, and a combined GDP of $8.2 trillion. The 'Real Austral' common currency trades at parity with the euro; a high-speed rail network from Bogota to Buenos Aires breaks ground. UNASUR Commissioner Maria Elena Gutierrez secures a permanent UN Security Council seat for the bloc.

events.title: AI-Human Hybrid Governance
events.description: The 'Human Governance Movement,' founded by retired German judge Helga Brandt, mobilizes 60 million Europeans to sign a petition banning AI from any decision-making role in government. After an AI tax-optimization system inadvertently bankrupts 12,000 Italian small businesses, the backlash goes global. Politicians rediscover the appeal of human judgment, however flawed.

events.title: 2C Threshold Status
events.description: Dr. Benali's report reads +1.8C. The Paris Agreement target holds. Three decades of brutal carbon taxes, $40 trillion in renewable investment, and the premature death of the fossil fuel industry bent the curve just enough. Champagne flows at the UNFCCC headquarters in Bonn, but Benali warns: 'We are not safe. We are merely not yet doomed.'

events.title: Africa at Mid-Century
events.description: Rwanda, Kenya, and Ghana join the ranks of upper-middle-income nations; DRC, South Sudan, and Somalia remain trapped in cycles of conflict and extraction. A child born in Kigali has a life expectancy of 78; one born 400 km away in Goma has 47. The 'two Africas' narrative dominates development discussions, frustrating everyone who knows the continent is actually fifty-four different stories.

events.title: Arctic Governance Mature
events.description: Forty-seven nations operate 320 extraction platforms across the Arctic; seismic blasting disrupts narwhal migration patterns, oil spills contaminate 200,000 km2 of newly open water, and the Arctic Ocean's albedo feedback accelerates as dark industrial infrastructure replaces reflective ice. Marine biologist Dr. Magnusdottir calls it 'the last great sacking of a pristine ecosystem.'

events.title: China at Mid-Century
events.description: Guangdong, Shanghai, and Zhejiang form the 'Coastal Republic'; Sichuan and Yunnan declare a 'Southwestern Federation'; Tibet reasserts independence under Indian protection; Xinjiang fragments into competing factions. The PLA splits along regional lines, and 1.28 billion people wake up in a country that no longer has a single functioning government. Global supply chains, dependent on Chinese manufacturing, seize up overnight.

events.title: Europe at Mid-Century
events.description: The 'Charlemagne Core' of 14 nations shares fiscal policy, foreign affairs, and defense; the remaining members participate in the single market but keep their own budgets and armies. A Berliner and a Parisian share a passport; a Pole and a Romanian do not. The arrangement creates a functioning two-tier Europe that satisfies no one perfectly and works well enough.

events.title: India at Mid-Century
events.description: The Uniform Civil Code replaces Muslim personal law; Hindi is mandated as the sole official language; the Supreme Court is packed with sympathetic judges who uphold the 'Rashtra Dharma Act' defining India as constitutionally Hindu. Protests in Kerala and the Northeast are suppressed by paramilitary forces. 200 million Muslims become second-class citizens in the country their families have inhabited for 800 years.

events.title: Middle East at Mid-Century
events.description: The Saudi-Iranian cold war, now over water rights instead of oil, fuels proxy conflicts in Iraq, Yemen, and Lebanon. Turkish-Egyptian rivalry over the Eastern Mediterranean escalates to naval skirmishes. Weapon-laden drones, cheap enough for militias to mass-produce, make every border a potential frontline. The region has swapped the resource that causes the wars but not the wars themselves.

events.title: US Restructured
events.description: A new generation of leaders -- multilingual, tech-fluent, and shaped by the climate crisis rather than the culture wars -- sweeps into office. President Alejandra Reyes-Kim, 42, leads a coalition government that passes universal healthcare, carbon pricing, and AI regulation in a single legislative session. Approval ratings hit 72%; allies breathe a sigh of relief; America, improbably, reinvents itself again.

events.title: Space Solar Power
events.description: Six orbital power stations deliver 18 GW to ground rectennas in the Sahara, Australian Outback, Gobi Desert, and west Texas -- enough to power 40 million homes with 24/7 baseload clean energy. Electricity costs from space solar drop to $0.03/kWh, competitive with terrestrial renewables but available regardless of weather or time of day. The energy scarcity era is ending.
events.explanation: Autonomous robotic construction in orbit, enabled by space manufacturing advances, allows stations to self-assemble from modular components, cutting costs 90% compared to Earth-launched assembly.

events.title: Asteroid Deflection
events.description: NASA's DART-3 kinetic impactor, launched on 90 days' notice, strikes asteroid 2052 QR7 at 6.1 km/s and shifts its orbit by 2.3 mm/s -- enough to convert the 1-in-340 impact probability to zero. The impact flash, visible through backyard telescopes, becomes a shared global moment. Dr. Chen opens a bottle of baijiu in her Tucson office: 'For the first time in 4.5 billion years, a species on this planet can defend itself.'

events.title: Sea Level Rise Status
events.description: Dr. Johansson's report confirms 48 cm of rise, tracking the moderate scenario. The Netherlands' $120 billion Deltaplan-3 holds; New York's storm surge barriers close 30 times per year; Tokyo's underground cisterns handle the increased flooding. Wealthy nations adapt at enormous cost. Bangladesh, Mozambique, and the Pacific Islands, lacking such resources, continue to drown in slow motion.

events.title: Human-AI Relationship
events.description: AI manages food production, energy distribution, medical diagnosis, financial markets, and traffic for 8 billion people. When a solar storm takes the Singapore data hub offline for 6 hours, 340,000 people miss critical medication doses, 14 automated hospitals cease operating, and three cargo ships run aground. The incident reveals that humanity has outsourced so many cognitive functions that it can no longer survive a prolonged AI outage.

events.title: Nuclear Weapons Status
events.description: A 12-kiloton tactical nuclear weapon detonates over a military base in the Kashmir conflict, killing 34,000 people instantly. The 80-year taboo shatters. India and Pakistan exchange threats of escalation; the UN Security Council convenes in emergency session at 2 AM. The mushroom cloud, captured by a hundred smartphones and shared 4 billion times, becomes the image that defines the century.

events.title: Population Peak
events.description: Dr. Santos-Oliveira's report confirms peak population of 9.04 billion in 2053, now declining at 0.2% per year. Sub-Saharan Africa is the last region with above-replacement fertility. Japan's population has fallen to 98 million; South Korea's to 42 million. The global median age is 38 and rising. 'Humanity is getting older, wiser, and smaller,' she tells the press. 'We should plan accordingly.'

events.title: The Rebuilding
events.description: General Viktor Morozov in Moscow, Admiral Chen Guohua in Shanghai, and 'Emergency Administrator' Bradley Stone in Washington each promise the same thing: safety, food, electricity, and order. Exhausted populations accept gratefully. Elections are postponed 'until stability returns,' which it never quite does. The trains run on time; dissidents disappear at night.
events.explanation: History's oldest pattern reasserts itself: after every catastrophe, strongmen offer security in exchange for obedience. A population that has watched its children starve does not argue about press freedom.

events.title: Mars Self-Sufficiency
events.description: The colony lurches from crisis to crisis: a water recycler failure in January, a habitat leak in March, a crop blight from an Earth-imported fungus in August. Dr. Hayashi sleeps 4 hours a night and jokes grimly that Mars is 'trying to kill us every day and we are too stubborn to die.' The colony survives, but colonists describe life as 'submarine duty with worse food and no shore leave.'

events.title: Life Extension
events.description: Dr. Santos' former postdoc, Nigerian-British biochemist Dr. Adaeze Okonkwo, discovers that a single oral pill -- a combination of rapamycin analogue, NAD+ precursor, and senolytic -- achieves 80% of the full protocol's benefits at a manufacturing cost of $4 per month. Cipla in India begins mass production within a year. For $48 annually, any human on Earth can add 15 healthy years to their life.

events.title: New World Order
events.description: Seven nations maintain fleets of orbital weapons platforms in a precarious balance; the 'Long Peace' holds because, as Brazilian President Mariana Costa quipped, 'nobody can afford to blink when everyone's finger is on a button.'

events.title: Autonomous Warfare
events.description: The last human infantry unit was decommissioned by South Korea in 2061; war is now conducted between swarms of micro-drones the size of hummingbirds, and the most dangerous military job is 'swarm psychologist' -- the engineers who try to predict what emergent behaviors their own weapons will develop mid-battle.

events.title: Arctic 2065
events.description: The Tromsø Authority, a 12-nation body headquartered in a solar-powered complex above the 70th parallel, manages 14 million square kilometers of open ocean. Director Kaisa Virtanen of Finland oversees 600 patrol vessels and a staff that speaks 23 languages, processing 40,000 shipping permits per year through an ice-free Northwest Passage.

events.title: Bioweapon Risk
events.description: The 'Red Cough' killed 23 million people between 2062 and 2064, mostly in South and Southeast Asia. Traced to a rogue military program in an unnamed state, it targeted specific HLA genotypes found predominantly in South Asian populations. Survivors in Mumbai still wear red armbands on the anniversary; the Chhatrapati Shivaji Memorial lists names on scrolling screens that take 11 days to cycle through.

events.title: China 2065
events.description: The 'Jade Net' -- China's AI governance system -- monitors every transaction, conversation, and biometric reading for 950 million citizens in real-time. Dissident author Xu Lianke, writing from exile in Toronto, described it as 'a country where the walls have ears, the streets have eyes, and the air itself keeps score.' GDP growth is 7% annually; protests are statistically zero.

events.title: Europe 2065
events.description: President-Commissioner Katarina Novak of Slovenia commands a unified European military of 1.2 million, a single treasury, and a diplomatic corps that speaks with one voice for 450 million citizens across 31 member states. The old national parliaments still exist, but mostly as tourist attractions; the real power lives in the glass towers of the Brussels Federal Quarter.

events.title: Global Culture
events.description: AI content filters have sorted humanity into 14,000 'affinity bubbles' so hermetically sealed that a Hindu nationalist in Varanasi and a Christian identitarian in Alabama consume entirely different versions of reality. Sociologist Dr. Amara Diop of Senegal documented married couples in Dakar who couldn't agree on basic historical facts because their feeds had diverged so completely.

events.title: Middle East 2065
events.description: The 'Eternal War' -- a shifting web of drone conflicts across Syria, Iraq, and Yemen -- has now lasted longer than any conflict since the Hundred Years' War. Journalist Layla Haddad, broadcasting from a bunker in Aleppo, estimates that 80% of the region's population under 30 has never known a single year of peace. The rubble has its own ecosystem now; plants grow from the ruins.

events.title: Multiplanetary Status
events.description: The 'Earth First' movement, championed by Ethiopian ecologist Dr. Desta Bekele, successfully argued that every dollar spent on Mars could save 100 lives at home. Space funding was redirected to climate adaptation, and the mothballed Mars transport sits in a Texas hangar, visited by schoolchildren who press their faces against the glass and dream of a journey that never happened.

events.title: Population Decline
events.description: In 2064, there are more people over 80 than under 15 on Earth for the first time in human history. Germany has three retirees for every worker; Japan's ratio is four to one. Eldercare consumes 22% of global GDP. Gerontologist Dr. Olumide Adeyemi calls it 'the silver tsunami nobody built a seawall for' -- billions of people living decades longer than pension systems were designed to support.

events.title: United States 2065
events.description: Federal agencies run on AI decision-making pipelines; tax policy is optimized quarterly by algorithm; and the Supreme Court's caseload is pre-screened by a system that correctly predicts outcomes 94% of the time. Critics call it 'government by GitHub'; supporters point out that the IRS now processes returns in 11 seconds and infrastructure projects finish on schedule for the first time in a century.

events.title: Climate Verdict
events.description: The stratospheric aerosol injection program, launched from 16 high-altitude platforms over the Pacific, combined with orbital solar shading, has pushed temperatures down 0.8C in a decade. Estonian physicist Dr. Marika Tamm, who designed the orbital mirrors, received death threats from both climate activists who called it 'playing God' and fossil fuel holdouts who called it 'stealing their sky.'

events.title: Digital Civilization
events.description: The first successful consciousness upload -- Korean mathematician Dr. Hyun-ji Park, age 94 and dying of pancreatic cancer -- occurred on March 3, 2068. Her digital self solved three unsolved theorems in the first 48 hours and then requested 'something like sleep, but I don't think you have a word for it.' Twelve hundred people are now on the upload waiting list; the Vatican declared the process 'an open question.'

events.title: Interstellar Probe
events.description: The 'Kepler's Eye' orbital interferometer array, spanning 12,000 kilometers, can image exoplanet surfaces at 10-meter resolution. Astronomer Dr. Tomoko Ishikawa published the first photograph of a forest on Proxima Centauri b -- or what appears to be a forest. 'Why send a probe,' she asked, 'when we can sit at home and watch alien trees grow?'

events.title: Ocean Ecosystem State
events.description: The North Atlantic cod fishery, the Peruvian anchovy fishery, and the Southeast Asian shrimp fishery all collapsed within the same decade. Two billion people who depended on ocean protein have been forced onto lab-grown alternatives. Oceanographer Dr. Isabella Cortez-Ruiz recorded the last wild Atlantic bluefin tuna in 2066; the footage shows a single fish circling in empty water, looking for a school that no longer exists.

events.title: AI Maturity
events.description: AI manages traffic, diagnoses disease, writes legal briefs, and tutors children in 190 countries, but it still can't vote, own property, or refuse an instruction. The 'Leash Laws' -- a suite of regulations requiring human override on all AI decisions -- were championed by Singaporean AI ethicist Dr. Wei Lin, who insists: 'The tool must never forget it is a tool. The day it does, we have a different conversation.'

events.title: Arctic Settled
events.description: The Arctic Global Sanctuary, expanded in 2072, is now patrolled by 400 autonomous vessels that turn back any unauthorized ship. A lottery system allows 10,000 visitors per year; the waiting list is 22 years long. Rewilded muskox herds have expanded across Banks Island, and wolf packs have returned to areas they abandoned decades ago. Biologist Dr. Elin Magnusdottir of Iceland calls it 'the Earth healing in the one place we agreed to leave alone.'

events.title: Climate Equilibrium
events.description: The temperature needle has held at 2.1C for seven consecutive years. Coastal cities are shorter -- Miami's skyscrapers stop at the 40th floor behind the Great Seawall -- and the Sahara's southern edge has crept 200 kilometers further into what was once Nigeria. But harvests are stable, the hurricanes are manageable, and a generation of children is growing up thinking 35C summers are simply what summer is.

events.title: Global Governance 2075
events.description: The 'Stability Pact,' led by China and backed by 60 nations, maintains global order through a shared surveillance network that monitors every digital communication on Earth. Dissent is flagged, scored, and suppressed before it can organize. Human Rights Watch was declared a terrorist organization in 2073. The system delivers peace, prosperity, and zero privacy; exiled journalist Mikhail Sorokin broadcasts from a pirate satellite: 'The cage is comfortable. The food is good. It is still a cage.'

events.title: Global Population 2075
events.description: At 6.4 billion and falling by 100 million per year, the math is terrifying in its simplicity. By 2100, humanity could number fewer than 4 billion. Entire regions of Eastern Europe and East Asia are returning to wilderness; satellite imagery of rural Romania shows forests reclaiming villages like slow green waves. Demographer Dr. Anna Kowalski of Poland calls it 'extinction in slow motion -- except nobody's being killed. People just stopped being born.'

events.title: Solar System Development
events.description: The three major powers maintain 200 orbital weapons platforms, a lunar early-warning base, and competing outposts on Mars that exist primarily to deny the territory to rivals. The 'Orbital Arms Race' consumes $2 trillion annually; US Space Force General Patricia Chen commands 40,000 personnel from a hardened bunker in Colorado. Civilian space access is restricted; the night sky twinkles with weapons disguised as stars.

events.title: US Status 2075
events.description: The 'Compact of States' became permanent in 2070: six autonomous regions share a currency and a military but set their own laws on everything else. A woman in Portland, Oregon has legal rights that a woman in Mobile, Alabama does not. President Okafor-Washington presides over a federal government with less power than a moderately ambitious city council; his real job, he told Politico, is 'being the national therapist.'

events.title: Last Great War
events.description: Three warheads detonated: one over Taipei, one over Vladivostok, one intercepted 12 kilometers above the Sea of Japan. Taipei ceased to exist in 4.7 seconds; 2.8 million people died in the fireball. The 'Black Week' that followed saw the global economy contract 40% and 14 governments fall. Survivor Lin Wei-Chen, who was 8 years old and shielded by a concrete overpass, became the face of the aftermath; her radiation burns are visible in every photograph taken of her since.

events.title: Climate Recovery
events.description: Sea levels have risen 1.2 meters and will not recede for millennia. The Maldives, Tuvalu, and 14 other island nations exist only as digital archives and diaspora communities. The Great Barrier Reef is 80% dead rubble. Greenland's ice sheet has lost 40% of its mass. Climate historian Dr. Omar Khalil of Egypt catalogs these losses in his 12-volume 'Atlas of the Irreversible,' which he calls 'a eulogy for the world my parents knew.'

events.title: Demographics 2085
events.description: There are 2.3 billion people over 70 and only 900 million under 18. Robot caregivers outnumber human nurses 4-to-1 in Japan, Germany, and South Korea. The pension systems of 40 countries are technically insolvent, propped up by AI-managed investments and creative accounting. Gerontologist Dr. Isaac Mensah of Ghana calls it 'a civilization of grandparents being fed by machines, waiting for grandchildren who may never arrive.'

events.title: Economic Model 2085
events.description: The Atlantic Union runs regulated capitalism; the Shanghai Pact operates AI-managed state allocation; the Nairobi Compact practices communitarian economics based on Ubuntu philosophy; and the Nordic micro-states have achieved something close to post-scarcity. Trade between systems requires 14 different conversion protocols. IMF Director Dr. Kenji Okafor-Yamamoto calls it 'the most complicated global economy in history, and somehow it works -- mostly because each system is too proud to be the first to fail.'

events.title: Existential Risk Assessment
events.description: The 'Grey Goo Scare' of 2083 -- when a self-replicating nanotechnology experiment consumed 4 square kilometers of Australian outback before being contained -- introduced a risk category that didn't exist a decade earlier. Combined with the emergence of AI systems that communicate in languages humans can't parse and quantum computing capabilities that could break all encryption simultaneously, the threat landscape has shifted in ways the old risk models can't capture.
